questions.promptTex: \text{For } y < 3x + 2, \text{ the boundary line should be drawn:}
questions.explanation: $<$ is **strict** — the boundary line itself is NOT part of the solution. So draw it **dashed**.

questions.promptTex: \text{For } y \geq -x + 1, \text{ the boundary line should be drawn:}
questions.explanation: $\geq$ is **non-strict** — points on the boundary *are* solutions. Draw the line **solid**.

questions.promptTex: \text{Test } (0, 0) \text{ in } y > 2x - 3.\\ \text{Does the origin satisfy the inequality?}
choices: Can't tell from the inequality alone
questions.explanation: Substitute: $0 > 2(0) - 3 = -3$ is **true** ($0 > -3$). So $(0, 0)$ is a solution → shade the side containing the origin.

questions.promptTex: \text{To graph } y > 2x, \text{ which side of the line should you shade?}
hints: The inequality is already solved for $y$.
questions.explanation: $y > 2x$ says the y-coordinate is *above* the line. Shade above. (Test it with $(0, 1)$: $1 > 0$ ✓.)

choices: It's the only valid test point
It's almost always the easiest to compute
It's required by the test-point method
hints: What happens when you plug zeros into $y \;\square\; mx + b$?
questions.explanation: Substituting $(0, 0)$ leaves only the constant on the right side of $y = mx + b$ — easiest possible arithmetic. The only time you can't use $(0, 0)$ is when the boundary line passes through the origin (then pick something else simple, like $(1, 0)$).

questions.promptTex: \text{Graphing } y > 2x + 1: \text{ the boundary line is and the shading is:}
steps: **Boundary type:** strict inequality ($<$ or $>$) → dashed; non-strict ($\leq$ or $\geq$) → solid.
**Shading:** $y > 2x + 1$ means $y$ is greater than the line — shade **above**.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which point is in the solution region of } y \leq -x + 3?
hints: Test each point in the inequality.

questions.explanation: Strict inequality ($<$) → dashed boundary (boundary points not included).
steps: $\leq$ or $\geq$ → non-strict → boundary included → solid line.

questions.promptTex: \text{Use the test point } (0, 0) \text{ to determine which side to shade for } y > 3x - 6.
hints: Shade the side that contains the test point.
questions.explanation: Test $(0, 0)$: $0 > -6$ ✓. Since true, shade the side containing $(0, 0)$.
steps: Since the test point satisfies the inequality, shade the side **containing** $(0, 0)$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which inequality has } (3, -1) \text{ on its boundary line?}
hints: Plug $(3, -1)$ into each: which makes the equality (boundary equation) true?
questions.explanation: Boundary of $y \geq 2x - 7$ is $y = 2x - 7$. Test: $-1 = 2(3) - 7 = -1$ ✓. The point lies on this boundary.
steps: A point is on the boundary iff it satisfies the equation form (with $=$).
Other options either don't satisfy the boundary equation or correspond to strict inequalities.

hints: All points to the right of (or on) the vertical line $x = 2$.
questions.explanation: $x \geq 2$ means $x$-coordinate is 2 or more — that's the region to the right of (and on) the vertical line $x = 2$.
steps: $x \geq 2$ includes all points with $x$-coordinate at least 2.
That's the region **to the right of (and on) the line** $x = 2$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which inequality has its solution as the entire upper half-plane (above the x-axis)?}
questions.explanation: $y > 0$ describes all points with positive $y$-coordinate, i.e., the upper half-plane (excluding the x-axis itself, since the inequality is strict).
